name: ESB eCars
alexConclusions:
ESB is already an established player since 2010.

Their focus is clearly on Destination Public Charging and fleet charging.

Because they are the biggest, there are many problems that EV drivers have to put up with for years, but they have no other choice.

ESB is using Driivz.

They actually have home charging through Electric Ireland with Zappi only. Driivz doesn't provide home charging, so that's why we don't see this being prompted much on the ESB side of the business.

They will most likely find it interesting having a full turn-key solution for fleets where they can provide public, workplace, but also home and residential charging with reimbursements. However, their current minimum requirement is — solar integration. Most likely even battery. In Ireland, a lot of smaller players actually provide home reimbursement and roaming for fleets.. So that's a big missed opportunity that is worth exploring.

Roaming is something that we didn't see being mentioned on their website. I know that they have roaming, but they are very closed. Probably a strategic decision, because they are one of the big players on a small market, so probably the perception is that user have to download their app anyways.

Actually, if they provided a better smoother service, most likely all EV drivers will default to their network.

If I see that customers are switching from ESB to EasyGo, we can use that to push for migration. In other words, if EasyGo was to provide way better service, ESB will be forced to look into other solutions.

businessType.label: Charging scope
businessType.value: Public destination + fleet / workplace. No home charging under ESB brand.

businessType.label: DC estimate
businessType.value: 140 DC locations — mix of legacy 50kW and newer 360kW high-power

businessType.label: AC estimate
businessType.value: Majority of 580 locations — legacy 22kW ABB-era stations

businessType.label: Home charging
businessType.value: Available via Electric Ireland (separate division) with Zappi only

overview.countriesNote: Multi-currency and multi-VAT operations — IE and GB legal entities managed separately.

composition.label: Total connectors
composition.value: 1,600 (per FAQ) — up to 1,400 simultaneous EVs per homepage claim

composition.label: AC hardware
composition.value: 22kW stations — large legacy estate from 2010 rollout

composition.label: Growth target
composition.value: 2,100 stations by 2030 — recent focus is high-power DC destination

roaming.name: Closed network
roaming.desc: Very limited roaming — confirmed by observation

roaming.name: RFID card
roaming.desc: Recommended by users over app in poor signal areas

network.roamingNote: Roaming is notably absent from their website. ESB operates a largely closed network — EV drivers on competitor networks have limited access. No major roaming hub integrations confirmed.

partnerships.label: eHubs
partnerships.value: Shared mobility hubs (cars, bikes, eCargo) — city-subsidised, on-street

app.label: Subscriptions
app.value: Members plan vs Pay-as-you-go (members get ~€0.05/kWh discount)

app.label: Fleet portal
app.value: Separate fleet management portal — vehicle tracking included

app.label: App health
app.value: Mixed to negative reviews — crashes, holding fees, map issues post-redesign

app.label: Support
app.value: 24/7 — most likely internal given company scale

product.appNote: Major user frustrations: €30–50 card pre-authorisation holds, €8 overstay fees after 45 min on rapid chargers, app crashes. RFID card recommended by experienced users for reliability.

stack.label: CPMS
stack.value: Driivz — confirmed via case study and app

stack.label: Smart energy
stack.value: Separate smart energy management software (not CPMS)

stack.label: Home charging
stack.value: Zappi (via Electric Ireland) — proprietary software, not integrated with eCars

siteHost.label: Target hosts
siteHost.value: Tesco, public car parks, transport hubs, city centres

siteHost.label: Revenue model
siteHost.value: ESB owns and operates — no standard rev-share model publicly stated

siteHost.label: Pricing model
siteHost.value: Per-kWh pricing: members €0.54–0.61/kWh, PAYG €0.59–0.66/kWh depending on speed

siteHost.label: Overstay fee
siteHost.value: €8 after 45 min on rapid chargers — contentious with users

revenue.label: Session fees
revenue.value: Per-kWh charging — members and PAYG tiers

revenue.label: Fleet contracts
revenue.value: Fleet portal + workplace charging for corporate clients

revenue.label: Overstay fees
revenue.value: €8/session after threshold — generates revenue but causes significant user friction

revenue.label: Utility parent
revenue.value: ESB Group — energy generation, grid (2.5M customers), retail (1.3M customers via Electric Ireland)

funding.label: Parent
funding.value: ESB Group — state-backed Irish energy utility

funding.label: Scale
funding.value: ESB Networks services 2.5M homes and businesses

funding.label: Investment pace
funding.value: Active: recent announcements focus on high-power DC destination hubs

policy.label: Market position
policy.value: Dominant Irish CPO — drivers often have no alternative despite pricing complaints

policy.label: GB expansion
policy.value: Growing presence in Great Britain, adds multi-currency/VAT/legal complexity

policy.label: Regulation
policy.value: State utility status gives strong positioning in public tenders and grants

salesSignals.text: Known CPMS: Driivz. Driivz does not support home charging — which is a confirmed gap. ESB is a utility that services millions of homes, making home charging a natural expansion they're currently missing.
App quality is a visible weak point. User reviews cite crashes, confusing maps, and payment friction. A branded app with better UX is a concrete differentiator to lead with.

Home charging gap. Electric Ireland offers Zappi only — no integrated home charging under the ESB eCars umbrella. A full-stack CPMS covering public + workplace + home with reimbursement is a clear unmet need.
Multi-entity complexity. Ireland + Great Britain means multiple currencies, VATs, and legal entities. This is a known CPMS feature that would resonate with their ops team.
Competitor pressure exists. If EasyGo or other Irish CPOs are winning drivers away from ESB due to pricing and reliability, that's a useful data point to leverage in outreach.

openQuestions: Is ESB actively evaluating alternatives to Driivz, or is the relationship locked in long-term?
Who owns the CPMS relationship at ESB — is it IT, the eCars division, or the utility parent?
Are drivers visibly switching to EasyGo or other networks? What does market share data show?
Does ESB have any plans to integrate home charging with eCars (beyond Electric Ireland's Zappi offering)?
What is the internal SLA performance on Driivz — are they satisfied with uptime and billing accuracy?

synthesis.verdictText: Dominant Irish CPO with a confirmed CPMS (Driivz), a visible home charging gap, app quality issues, and multi-country complexity. The case for switching centres on the home charging use case, better driver UX, and integrated fleet + public + workplace management. Timing depends on Driivz contract length.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 1 — Home charging is the wedge
hypotheses.text: Driivz's lack of home charging is a structural gap for a company that serves 1.3M homes via Electric Ireland. A CPMS that unifies public, workplace, and home with driver reimbursements would be a material upgrade — and a story ESB could tell to enterprise fleet clients.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 2 — App quality is the visible pain point
hypotheses.text: User complaints about the app are public and consistent. A better-branded, more reliable driver app is a low-risk conversation opener that doesn't require internal CPMS knowledge upfront.

hypotheses.label: Hypothesis 3 — Fleet expansion creates new software requirements
hypotheses.text: Their fleet portal currently supports vehicle tracking, but fleet-as-a-service (home reimbursement, mixed public/workplace sessions, multi-depot reporting) will require capabilities beyond what legacy Driivz provides as they grow.

uncertainties: Driivz contract terms unknown — could be multi-year with significant switching costs.
Decision-making split between ESB eCars, Electric Ireland, and the ESB Group IT function makes identifying the right buyer complex.
ESB's state utility status may mean procurement decisions go through long public tender processes.

contacts.name: eCars leadership
contacts.title: Head of eCars / EV Strategy
contacts.note: Identify current head via LinkedIn — role may sit under ESB Networks or as a standalone division.

synthesis.contactsNote: Also look for: CTO or Head of Digital at ESB Group; Head of Fleet Solutions. The home charging angle may resonate more with Electric Ireland's product team.
